JavaScript Objects: How to Read, Add, and Delete Properties
Next up, let's talk about working with objects — reading values out of them, adding new properties, and deleting them.

One more thing to watch out for: object keys are
strings. So in the sample code there's a number

. So you might think, okay, let me try
. So what's actually going on here? Let's take
as the example and look at how Chrome's Console renders it.
Say you're typing out a plain Number — it shows up in this light green color:
But if you use dot notation and type a number after it, it ends up looking like this:
dot notation followed by a number
With that approach, you can see that whatever comes after the
gets treated as light green and is mistaken for a Number. That's why I'd recommend using bracket notation to read these values.
Now you might ask: why is it that when you write JavaScript, you

have to wrap object keys in quotes? The main reason is this — even though object keys can only ever be strings, anything that isn't a string gets coerced into one. And because of that coercion, when we're coding we usually just leave the quotes off and lean on JavaScript's string coercion to write our objects.

The idea here is pretty much the same as reading values — you still go through dot notation or bracket notation.
